message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-9, latest commit). Confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Be skeptical; find holes. For a constrained finding (CI-1) judge the response given the real constraint.

FINDING P9-MO-1:
confirm primitives raised an opaque TypeError when alpha was locked on NEITHER the hypothesis NOR the call (resolved to None → 0.0 < None). Fix in src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py _validate_alpha: reject None with a ValueError naming alpha and how to provide it. VERIFY: verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=None, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True) raises ValueError (not TypeError) matching "alpha is required". Test: test_alpha_none_raises_clear_valueerror_not_opaque_typeerror. Confirm the existing valid-alpha tests still pass.

Read the changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>; "python -m pytest" not bare pytest; gh available for CI-1). Judge ONLY P9-MO-1. CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if a real residual gap; REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll review finding P9-MO-1. Let me start by examining the changed file and tests.

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence, cast
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float | None) -> None:
84	    # alpha is REQUIRED — locked on the hypothesis or supplied at the call. When neither is given,
85	    # _resolve_locked_param returns None; reject it here with a clear ValueError naming alpha rather
86	    # than letting the `0.0 < None` comparison below raise an opaque TypeError (#P9-MO-1).
87	    if alpha is None:
88	        raise ValueError(
89	            "alpha is required: lock it on the hypothesis (Hypothesis(alpha=...)) or pass alpha= "
90	            "to the confirm primitive"
91	        )
92	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
93	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
94	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
95	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
96	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
97	
98	
99	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
100	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
101	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
102	
103	
104	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
105	    if not math.isfinite(value):
106	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
107	
108	
109	def verdict_from_pvalue(
110	    hypothesis_id: str,
111	    *,
112	    statistic: float,
113	    p_value: float,
114	    alpha: float,
115	    powered: bool,
116	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
117	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
118	) -> Verdict:
119	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
120	
121	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
122	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
123	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
124	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
125	
126	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
127	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
128	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
129	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
130	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
131	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
132	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
133	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
134	
135	    ``powered`` is likewise a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION (pass 5, #H-002): the engine cannot
136	    compute statistical power from an opaque statistic/p-value, so the caller asserts whether the
137	    test was adequately powered (``powered=False`` forces ``indeterminate``). The engine cannot
138	    verify it but records it in ``evidence`` so the verdict's dependence on the flag is auditable.
139	    """
140	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
141	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
142	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
143	
144	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
145	    common = dict(
146	        statistic=statistic,
147	        threshold=alpha,
148	        evidence={
149	            "p_value": p_value,
150	            "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim,
151	            "powered": powered,  # trusted caller flag, recorded for audit (#H-002)
152	        },
153	    )
154	    if not powered:
155	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
156	    if p_value <= alpha:
157	        if direction_supports_claim:
158	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
159	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
160	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
161	
162	
163	def confirm_unit_level(
164	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
165	    *,
166	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
167	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
168	    statistic: float,
169	    p_value: float,
170	    alpha: float,
171	    powered: bool,
172	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
173	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
174	) -> Verdict:
175	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
176	
177	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
178	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
179	
180	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
181	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
182	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
183	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
184	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
185	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
186	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
187	
188	    Consequently (pass 5, #H-015) the unit-level path deliberately does NOT mirror
189	    :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`'s directionless-lock REFUSAL: because the locked direction never
190	    drives the unit-level verdict, a directionless lock here is harmless, not a HARKing vector —
191	    the asymmetry is intentional, not a missing guard.
192	
193	    Test identity (pass 5, #H-003): ``test_name`` is bound into the pre-registration proof for
194	    audit, but the engine receives only an opaque ``statistic``/``p_value`` and CANNOT verify that
195	    the test actually executed matches the locked ``test_name`` — that remains a caller obligation.
196	    """
197	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
198	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
199	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
200	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
201	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
202	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
203	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
204	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
205	    # against an unrecognized tail.
206	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
207	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
208	    ):
209	        raise ValueError(
210	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
211	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
212	        )
213	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
214	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
215	    alpha = cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.alpha, alpha, "alpha"))  # #H-001
216	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
217	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
218	        statistic=statistic,
219	        p_value=p_value,
220	        alpha=alpha,
221	        powered=powered,
222	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
223	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
224	    )
225	
226	
227	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
228	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
229	    n = len(null)
230	    if tail == "greater":
231	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
232	    else:
233	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
234	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
235	
236	
237	def _resample_stability(
238	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
239	) -> float:
240	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
241	
242	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
243	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
244	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
245	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
246	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
247	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
248	
249	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
250	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
251	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
252	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
253	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
254	    """
255	    n = len(null)
256	    try:
257	        import numpy as np
258	
259	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
260	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
261	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
262	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
263	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
264	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
265	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
266	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
267	        if tail == "greater":
268	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
269	        else:
270	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
271	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
272	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
273	    except ImportError:
274	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
275	        return significant / len(resamples)
276	
277	
278	def _resolve_locked_param(locked: float | None, supplied: float | None, name: str) -> float | None:
279	    """Cross-check a confirm-time threshold against the pre-registered one (pass 5, #H-001).
280	
281	    If the hypothesis locked the value, the confirm-time argument may only AGREE with it (a
282	    mismatch raises) — the analyst cannot pick alpha / the stability bar after seeing the data
283	    (threshold HARKing). The locked value wins. If nothing was locked, the supplied value stands
284	    (exploratory / opted-out use), mirroring how direction worked before it could be locked.
285	    """
286	    if locked is not None:
287	        if supplied is not None and supplied != locked:
288	            raise ValueError(
289	                f"{name} argument {supplied!r} contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
290	                f"{name} {locked!r} — the threshold is fixed at lock time (no post-hoc choice)"
291	            )
292	        return locked
293	    return supplied
294	
295	
296	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
297	
298	
299	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
300	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
301	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
302	
303	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
304	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
305	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
306	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
307	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
308	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
309	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
310	
311	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
312	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
313	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
314	    """
315	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
316	    if locked_dir is None:
317	        raise ValueError(
318	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
319	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
320	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
321	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
322	        )
323	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
324	        raise ValueError(
325	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
326	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
327	        )
328	    resolved = locked_dir
329	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
330	        raise ValueError(
331	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
332	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
333	        )
334	    return resolved
335	
336	
337	def confirm_whole_corpus(
338	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
339	    *,
340	    observed: float,
341	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
342	    alpha: float,
343	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
344	    stability_threshold: float | None = None,
345	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
346	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
347	) -> Verdict:
348	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
349	
350	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
351	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
352	
353	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
354	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
355	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
356	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
357	
358	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
359	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
360	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
361	
362	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
363	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
364	    """
365	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
366	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
367	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
368	    # Cross-check the confirm-time thresholds against any pre-registered ones (#H-001): a locked
369	    # alpha / stability bar cannot be overridden post-hoc.
370	    alpha = cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.alpha, alpha, "alpha"))
371	    # stability_threshold's confirm-time default is None (a sentinel), NOT 0.9 (#CV-M-1): a fixed
372	    # 0.9 default was indistinguishable from a caller explicitly passing 0.9, so a hypothesis that
373	    # locked a threshold != 0.9 ALWAYS raised a spurious "contradicts pre-registered" error unless
374	    # the caller restated it. Resolve locked-wins-else-supplied, then fall back to 0.9 only when
375	    # neither was given.
376	    resolved_stability = _resolve_locked_param(
377	        hypothesis.stability_threshold, stability_threshold, "stability_threshold"
378	    )
379	    stability_threshold = 0.9 if resolved_stability is None else resolved_stability
380	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
381	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
382	    if not null_distribution:
383	        raise ValueError(
384	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
385	        )
386	    for v in null_distribution:
387	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
388	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
389	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
390	
391	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
392	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
393	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
394	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
395	    n = len(null_distribution)
396	
397	    stability: float | None = None
398	    contra_stability: float | None = None
399	    if resample_statistics is not None:
400	        if not resample_statistics:
401	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
402	        for r in resample_statistics:
403	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
404	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
405	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
406	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
407	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
408	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
409	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
410	        )
411	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
412	
413	    rule = (
414	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
415	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
416	    )
417	    evidence = {
418	        "p_support": p_support,
419	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
420	        "null_n": n,
421	        "predicted_direction": direction,
422	        "stability": stability,
423	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
424	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
425	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
426	    }
427	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
428	
429	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
430	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
431	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
432	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
433	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
434	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
435	    if p_contra <= alpha:
436	        return Verdict.contradicted(
437	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
438	        )
439	    if stability is None:
440	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
441	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
442	            rule,
443	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
444	            **common,
445	        )
446	    if not stable:
447	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
448	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
449	        )
450	    if p_support <= alpha:
451	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
452	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
453	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
454	    )
455

         )
 
 
-def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
+def _validate_alpha(alpha: float | None) -> None:
+    # alpha is REQUIRED — locked on the hypothesis or supplied at the call. When neither is given,
+    # _resolve_locked_param returns None; reject it here with a clear ValueError naming alpha rather
+    # than letting the `0.0 < None` comparison below raise an opaque TypeError (#P9-MO-1).
+    if alpha is None:
+        raise ValueError(
+            "alpha is required: lock it on the hypothesis (Hypothesis(alpha=...)) or pass alpha= "
+            "to the confirm primitive"
+        )
     # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
     # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
     # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.

125	            powered=True,
126	            direction_supports_claim=False,
127	        ).label

Verification complete. All three entry paths converge through `_validate_alpha`:

All paths are covered. input.rationale: Fix in src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py _validate_alpha (lines 83-91) now accepts alpha: float | None and rejects None with ValueError("alpha is required: lock it on the hypothesis ... or pass alpha= ...") BEFORE the 0.0 < alpha < 0.5 comparison that previously raised an opaque TypeError. Correct root cause (None resolution from _resolve_locked_param when alpha locked on neither hypothesis nor call), sound approach, and the right layer: _validate_alpha is the single shared validator on all three entry paths (verdict_from_pvalue line 141; confirm_unit_level resolves then routes through verdict_from_pvalue; confirm_whole_corpus resolves at line 370 and validates at line 380), so no path can reach a comparison with None. VERIFY reproduced: verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=None, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True) raises ValueError matching 'alpha is required'. The regression test test_alpha_none_raises_clear_valueerror_not_opaque_typeerror (tests/test_confirm.py:132) DISCRIMINATES: it uses pytest.raises(ValueError, match='alpha is required'); TypeError is not a subclass of ValueError, so reverting the fix (letting 0.0 < None raise TypeError, confirmed at runtime) would not be caught and the test fails. Full suite: 45 passed, including all valid-alpha and the parametrized invalid-alpha (0.0/0.5/1.0) cases.

content.text: CONFIRMED — P9-MO-1 fix is both appropriate and complete, with a discriminating regression test and all 45 tests passing.
